claims.text: On November 22, 1987 in Chicago an unknown 'pirate' hijacked WGN-TV's broadcast signal during a sportscast and replaced it with bizarre footage of a person wearing a Max Headroom rubber mask — and two hours later did the same thing to PBS station WTTW during a Doctor Who episode, marking one of the most bizarre unsolved crimes in television history.

the max headroom incident
wgn tv was able to act quickly and get
their signal back
but two hours later it would happen
again to another tv station
and

claims.rationale: The Max Headroom signal hijack is a well-documented FCC-investigated event from November 22, 1987. Both intrusions on WGN-TV (during the 9pm news with Dan Roan) and approximately two hours later on WTTW (PBS, Channel 11, during a Doctor Who 'Horror of Fang Rock' broadcast) are confirmed by FCC records, contemporaneous news coverage, and surviving recordings of both intrusions. The 'unsolved' framing is also accurate — no perpetrator has ever been publicly identified.

claims.text: WGN engineers initially thought the perpetrator was an inside job by an employee, but tearing apart the studio found no one — the pirate broadcast was pre-recorded and pushed onto the WGN signal by a third party who intercepted the studio-to-antenna uplink to the John Hancock Building.

claims.rationale: The 'inside job' working theory and subsequent search of the studio is documented in the WGN engineering team's contemporary statements. The Hancock Building did house WGN's microwave studio-transmitter-link path; the technical theory of intercepting the STL uplink between studio and the antenna at the Hancock is the FCC-accepted explanation. The 'pre-recorded' framing is correct — the audio/video has the signature of a tape playback, not live transmission.

claims.text: The second intrusion on PBS WTTW Channel 11 — interrupting Doctor Who in the middle of the night when no engineers were on duty — played all the way through with bizarre theatrical content (the masked figure ranting, hummed cartoon themes, getting spanked with a flyswatter) before the signal was returned, prompting joint FCC and FBI investigation that would 'turn the entire television industry upside down'.

claims.rationale: WTTW's overnight Doctor Who 'Horror of Fang Rock' broadcast was interrupted at approximately 11:15 PM CST (closer to two hours after the WGN incident at 9:14pm) and the intrusion played for ~90 seconds without WTTW staff on hand to interrupt. The bizarre content (masked figure, theatrical antics, flyswatter spanking) is on the surviving recording. FCC and FBI did open joint investigations.

claims.text: Despite popular impression, the real Max Headroom character was not computer-generated — he was actor Matt Frewer in latex prosthetics in front of a blue screen — and the original 1985 cyberpunk TV movie 'Max Headroom: 20 Minutes Into the Future' was deliberately conceived as satire of TV news where the line between news and entertainment was blurring.

claims.rationale: All historical claims are accurate: 'Max Headroom: 20 Minutes Into the Future' aired on Channel 4 UK in April 1985; the ABC US series ran 1987-88; Miami Vice did dominate the same time slot leading to the cancellation; Matt Frewer played the character with prosthetics on a blue screen, not CGI; the 'parody of newscasters where news/entertainment line blurs' was the explicit satirical brief from creators George Stone, Annabel Jankel, and Rocky Morton.

claims.text: The Max Headroom fictional universe — where television networks rule a dystopian government and TVs cannot be turned off so viewers' physical movements and thoughts are monitored — turned out in 1987 to predict the surveillance-and-attention economy we now live with under Facebook and Google.

claims.rationale: The substantive parallel between Max Headroom's fictional 'TVs that monitor thoughts and movements' and modern surveillance capitalism (smart TV ACR data collection, microphone-equipped streaming devices, algorithmic attention engineering by Meta/Alphabet) is genuine and has been remarked on by media scholars (e.g. Henry Jenkins, Shoshana Zuboff). Calling it a precise prediction is a stretch, but as cultural critique the parallel holds up.

claims.text: The pirate's robotic voice was achieved by running the audio through an analog ring modulator — the same technique used to create the Daleks' voice in Doctor Who — suggesting (along with the choice to hijack a Doctor Who episode) the perpetrator was likely a Doctor Who fan with sound engineering knowledge.

claims.rationale: The ring-modulator-Dalek-voice technical fact is correct (the Dalek voice was originally created with an EMS VCS3 ring modulator). The intrusion's voice does have the characteristic ring-modulated metallic quality. The inference that the perpetrator was a Doctor Who fan is reasonable but circumstantial — the ring modulator was a common piece of analog audio gear in any 1980s music studio. The 'hijacked a Doctor Who episode' could equally have been opportunism (PBS overnight = no staff to interrupt).

claims.text: Hijacking a major-market TV station signal in 1987 was considered nearly impossible (rare, requiring specialist knowledge and hundreds of thousands of dollars in equipment) — but the perpetrator exploited the unencrypted studio-transmitter-link microwave path to the Hancock Building antenna by simply parking equipment with line-of-sight to the tower and overpowering the legitimate signal.

claims.rationale: Pre-1987 broadcast signal intrusions were genuinely rare and the FCC did consider major-market stations relatively secure. The studio-transmitter-link (STL) attack vector — line-of-sight microwave to the Hancock antenna with sufficient ERP to overpower the legitimate STL — is the FCC-accepted technical reconstruction of how the Max Headroom hijack worked. STL signals were unencrypted at the time. The 'cheap equipment with line of sight' framing is technically accurate; modern STLs are encrypted.

claims.text: The Max Headroom hack was directly inspired by Captain Midnight, who exactly one year earlier on April 27, 1986 became 'America's first known broadcast signal intrusion' when satellite technician John MacDougall in Ocala, Florida flipped his uplink dish toward HBO during The Falcon and the Snowman to protest HBO's price hike that was hurting his satellite-TV-equipment business.

claims.rationale: All facts here are verifiable: April 27, 1986 around 12:32am EDT; HBO's broadcast of The Falcon and the Snowman; Captain Midnight color bars message protesting the $19.95 HBO encryption charge; perpetrator identified within days as John R. MacDougall, satellite uplink operator at Central Florida Teleport in Ocala. He was charged with transmitting without a license, fined $5,000, and lost his license for one year. The 'first known' framing is correct in US broadcasting context. The MacDougall-as-inspiration claim for Max is plausible but unproved.

claims.text: MacDougall (Captain Midnight) does not regret his hijack and was 'one hundred percent right' — HBO was deliberately gouging satellite customers in order to force them to switch to cable, which is exactly what happened.

claims.rationale: MacDougall's stated motive — that HBO's $19.95 monthly satellite descrambler fee was anticompetitive pricing aimed at killing the home-satellite market in favor of cable — is consistent with the actual industry trajectory: by the early 1990s the home C-band satellite TV market collapsed largely due to encryption-driven cost increases. The exact intent of HBO management is harder to establish; competitive analysis at the time noted price-discrimination concerns.

dependencies.rationale: Provides the perpetrator's own retrospective justification for the precedent hijack.

claims.text: Despite an FCC/FBI investigation, the Max Headroom case eventually went cold because the agencies weren't prepared for this type of investigation and didn't cooperate with each other — and to this day no perpetrator has come forward, with the prevailing theory being a disgruntled WGN employee with an axe to grind.

claims.rationale: Both points are well-established in the case's enduring literature: the joint FCC-FBI investigation went cold within months due to lack of physical evidence and inter-agency turf disputes (FCC saw it as unauthorized broadcasting; FBI saw it as a low-priority prank), and the 'disgruntled WGN insider' or 'former engineer with technical knowledge' theory remains the most-cited working hypothesis. A 2010-era Reddit thread surfaced new candidate identifications that were investigated and inconclusive.

claims.text: The most likely explanation for the Max Headroom hijack is that there was no political message and no motive — it was done purely for curiosity and the glory of pulling it off, and that's an answer AJ finds satisfying.

claims.rationale: The 'no message, no motive, just curiosity' framing is one of two leading interpretations (the other being a deniable inside-job grievance against WGN management). The intrusion content lacks a coherent political demand, which supports the prank/curiosity reading. AJ's 'good enough for me' is a values judgment about epistemic closure — acknowledging that some mysteries are best left as mysteries; this is not a falsifiable claim about the hijack itself.

dependencies.rationale: Picks the curiosity hypothesis over the disgruntled-employee hypothesis.
